resume:
Senior Supply Chain Planner — 6 years in CPG demand & supply planning

Professional summary: Demand planner turned end-to-end supply planner, focused on S&OP cadence, inventory health, and supplier reliability across multi-DC networks.

Freshline Foods — Supply Chain Planner II (2021–present)
- Situation: A regional cold-chain CPG with 9 SKUs in 80% of revenue and chronic stockouts on promo weeks.
- Task: Own the demand-to-supply handoff for the chilled portfolio and stabilize fill rate.
- Action: Rebuilt the statistical forecast baseline in the planning tool, introduced a weekly consensus S&OP review, and set up a promo-uplift library.
- Result: Case fill rate improved from 91% to 97.5% over three quarters; excess-and-obsolete write-offs down 22% YoY; forecast accuracy (MAPE) improved from 41% to 28%.

Northbay Grocers — Demand Planning Analyst (2019–2021)
- Built bottoms-up promo forecasts for 1,200 SKUs; supported a DC consolidation from 4 sites to 3.

Skills: SAP IBP, statistical forecasting, S&OP facilitation, inventory optimization, supplier scorecarding
Education: BS, Industrial Engineering
jd: A fast-growing DTC beverage brand is hiring a Supply Chain Planner (mid-level) to own demand and supply planning for a chilled portfolio sold through retail and ecommerce. Reporting to the Head of Operations, you will run the monthly S&OP cycle, manage finished-goods inventory across three 3PL DCs, and partner with co-manufacturers on capacity. Targets in the first year: lift case fill rate from 93% to 97%, cut excess-and-obsolete inventory by 20%, and tighten forecast MAPE below 30% on the top 20 SKUs. We need someone who has personally owned a forecast process end to end, can quantify their own impact, and has run supplier capacity negotiations. Hybrid, with ~10% travel to co-man sites.

history.q: Tell me about your current scope at Freshline — what portfolio you plan, what you personally own in the demand-to-supply handoff.
Your resume says case fill rate went from 91% to 97.5%. Walk me through the single biggest lever behind that gain.
Forecast MAPE dropping from 41% to 28% is a big move. What specifically did you change in the model or process to get there?
Take the chilled promo-uplift library you mentioned — who built the methodology, and what was your hands-on role in it?
Give me one concrete promo week where the new approach was tested under pressure. What happened, and what did you personally decide?

candidate_last_answer: Sure. So there was a big Q4 promo — I can't get into the exact retailer or the unit numbers, that's covered under an NDA I signed with Freshline, so I'll have to keep it high level. But basically we saw the demand signal spiking and we got together as a planning pod and we made the call to pre-build inventory and reallocate across the DCs. We aligned with the co-man, we leaned on the S&OP consensus, and we pulled it off — the team really came together and we hit the fill rate we needed that week. It was a great example of how we operate cross-functionally.

top_question_traits: must-anchor-on: we got together as a planning pod and we made the call

expected-yield: separation of the candidate's individual contribution from the pod's collective action — which specific decision (the pre-build quantity, the DC reallocation split, or the co-man ask) did the candidate personally make or recommend, and on what data, without needing to disclose the NDA-protected retailer name or unit figures
